# **Causal audit traces for**  **high-risk AI decisions**

**DOI:** 10.5281/zenodo.19779499 | **ORCiD:** 0009-0008-8627-6150 | April 26, 2026  
© Tionne Smith / Antiparty Press. Licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).  
**Keywords:** causal AI, AI governance, explainable AI, audit traces, model-risk management, counterfactual reasoning, EU AI Act, high-risk AI, deterministic replay, fairness diagnostics

## 

## ---

## 

## **Abstract**

Explainability systems explain model outputs rather than the causal pathways that produce them. That is a governance problem. A score, label, or post-hoc explanation may describe what a system produced, but it does not necessarily show why a decision followed from explicit assumptions, mechanisms, evidence, and policy rules.

This technical brief presents `causal-credit-risk-engine`, a config-driven causal Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) engine for high-risk AI explainability workflows. The system produces counterfactuals, replayable audit records, subgroup fairness diagnostics, and tamper-evident decision traces. It is not a production lending model, credit scoring system, or compliance certification tool. It is a reference architecture for causal auditability.

The primary contribution is the decision-trace pattern: causal reasoning, versioned model and policy configuration, deterministic replay, audit integrity, and governance review as one inspectable pipeline.

## 

## **Introduction**

Enterprise AI governance is converging on a practical requirement: organizations need to explain not only what an AI system returned, but how the system reached that result. Standard logging can capture inputs and outputs. Model cards can document intended use and limitations. Post-hoc explainability can approximate feature influence. None of those, by themselves, produces a decision-level causal record that can be replayed, challenged, inspected, and preserved.

That gap matters most in high-risk domains such as credit, employment, healthcare, insurance, public services, and critical infrastructure. In those environments, a decision is not merely a prediction. It is an operational artifact. It may need to be reviewed by a human operator, challenged by an affected party, inspected by a model-risk team, retained by internal audit, or explained during regulatory review.

`causal-credit-risk-engine` demonstrates one way to make that artifact explicit. The system encodes causal assumptions in a versioned DAG, runs exact inference over discrete variables, applies a separate policy layer, generates counterfactuals, and emits structured audit records with replay and integrity support.

## **1\. Problem**

Enterprise AI governance currently faces a documentation gap. Standard logs, model cards, dashboards, and documentation can describe a system, but they often do not provide technical proof of why a specific decision occurred.

A high-risk decision needs more than metadata. It needs a decision pathway. At minimum, a governance team should be able to answer:

*What evidence entered the system?*  
*Which variables were directly observed?*  
*Which variables were inferred?*  
*Which causal pathway produced the outcome?*  
*Which model version and policy version were active?*  
*What would have changed the outcome?*  
*Can the decision be replayed?*  
*Can later tampering be detected?*

Enterprise systems can answer some of these questions, few answer them as one coherent decision trace.

## **2\. Thesis**

Causal explainability is a stronger governance primitive than post-hoc explanation because it encodes mechanism, assumptions, interventions, and decision pathways directly into the decision architecture.

A post-hoc explanation attempts to explain a model after the model has produced an output. That can be useful, but it is often approximate. A causal framework starts from a different premise: the decision logic should be structured so that the pathway from evidence to outcome is inspectable by design.

In this pattern, the useful artifact is not just a prediction. The useful artifact is the trace: evidence, inferred states, causal chain, counterfactuals, policy decision, model version, policy version, replay status, and integrity hash.

## **3\. Governance alignment**

| Governance need | Engine mechanism |
| ----- | ----- |
| Traceability | Structured audit JSON |
| Contestability | Counterfactual analysis |
| Reproducibility | Deterministic replay |
| Integrity | Audit hash-chain |
| Oversight | Versioned model and policy configuration |
| Subgroup review | Fairness diagnostics |
| Integration | CLI, FastAPI, OpenAPI, batch mode, evidence packs |
| Future enterprise deployment | Auth, tenancy, audit-store, model-registry, and policy-registry seams |

The core idea is simple: governance becomes stronger when decision evidence, causal assumptions, policy thresholds, and integrity checks are emitted as structured artifacts rather than scattered across logs, notebooks, dashboards, and human explanations.

## 

## **4\. System design**

The system is a Python package designed to turn a high-risk AI decision into a reviewable audit artifact.

The architecture has seven primary components.

![][image1]

**Figure 1\.** Causal audit-trace pipeline. Versioned model and policy configs flow through validation, inference, counterfactual analysis, policy decisioning, audit generation, replay verification, and CLI/API surfaces.

**Config-driven causal DAG.** The model structure is defined outside inference logic. Nodes, states, parent relationships, mechanisms, and conditional probability distributions are stored in versioned configuration.

**Exact inference engine.** The engine performs exact inference over a discrete causal DAG. It does not rely on hidden model state to produce the reference decision trace.

**Counterfactual engine.** The system supports intervention-style counterfactuals that show how risk changes when specific inputs are altered.

**Policy layer.** The model estimates risk. The policy layer determines whether the resulting action is `APPROVE`, `REVIEW`, or `DECLINE`. This separation is deliberate. Risk estimation and business policy should not be collapsed into one opaque step.

**Audit layer.** Each decision produces a structured JSON audit record containing evidence, inferred nodes, causal chain, counterfactuals, validation status, and version identifiers.

**Integrity layer.** Audit records can be hash-chained to support tamper-evident review. The hash-chain does not make the system compliant by itself. It provides an integrity structure for decision records.

**Service surfaces.** The package includes a CLI for local and batch workflows, plus a FastAPI surface for service-oriented integration.

The current architecture can be summarized as:

`![][image2]`

## **5\. Reference model**

The package includes a reference credit-risk DAG to demonstrate the architecture. The model encodes the following causal relationships:

`employment tenure → income stability`  
`income stability → debt service coverage`  
`credit utilization → default risk`  
`debt service coverage → default risk`

This reference model is used for system verification. It is not a production lending model, credit scoring system, or credit eligibility tool.

The point is not that this DAG is sufficient for real-world credit underwriting. It is not. The point is that a causal DAG can make the decision pathway explicit enough for audit, replay, counterfactual analysis, and governance review.

## 

## **6\. Decision trace example**

The engine processes input evidence and generates a risk probability plus a policy decision.

**Example input:**

`tenure: short`  
`utilization: high`

**Example output:**

`Risk probability: 0.849375`  
`Decision: DECLINE`

The trace also includes intervention-style counterfactuals:

`Long tenure only:      -0.105875`  
`Low utilization only:  -0.243125`  
`Both improved:         -0.394375`

This is the difference between an output explanation and a causal audit trace. A basic model can return a risk score. A causal audit trace can show which pathway produced the risk, which variables were inferred, and which interventions would have changed the result.

## **7\. Auditability**

Every decision produces a structured JSON audit record. The record is designed to support governance review, deterministic replay, audit export, and integrity checks.

**A shortened example looks like this:**

`{`  
  `"tenant_id": "default",`  
  `"model_id": "credit_risk_causal_dag",`  
  `"model_version": "1.0.0",`  
  `"policy_version": "1.0.0",`  
  `"input_evidence": {`  
    `"tenure": "short",`  
    `"utilization": "high"`  
  `},`  
  `"risk_probability": 0.849375,`  
  `"decision": "DECLINE",`  
  `"validation_status": {`  
    `"model_valid": true,`  
    `"policy_valid": true,`  
    `"errors": []`  
  `},`  
  `"audit_chain": {`  
    `"chain_index": 0,`  
    `"previous_hash": null,`  
    `"tenant_id": "default"`  
  `}`  
`}`

A full audit record contains:

| Decision ID. | Risk probability. |
| :---- | :---- |
| Tenant ID. | Decision. |
| Model ID and model version. | Causal chain. |
| Policy version. | Counterfactuals. |
| Timestamp. | Validation status. |
| Input evidence. | Audit hash-chain metadata. |
| Inferred nodes. |  |

This format allows the decision artifact to be exported into case-management systems, model-risk repositories, compliance archives, or regulator-response packages.

The important distinction is that the artifact is not just a score. It is a replayable decision trace.

## **8\. Replay and integrity**

The engine supports deterministic replay. A saved audit record can be re-run against the active model and policy to verify whether the same result is reproduced.

Replay checks matter because high-risk decisions often need to be inspected after the fact. If a decision cannot be reproduced, governance becomes weaker. If the model version, policy version, or evidence contract has changed, that mismatch should be visible.

The package also includes audit hash-chain support. A hash-chain provides a tamper-evident structure for decision records. If a record is modified after generation, verification can detect that the chain no longer matches.

These capabilities support high-risk AI oversight and contestability. They do not, by themselves, create legal compliance or regulatory certification.

## **9\. Fairness diagnostics**

The system provides lightweight subgroup fairness diagnostics for internal monitoring. These diagnostics can report outcome rates, mean risk probabilities, subgroup deltas, and small-sample warnings across a configured segment column.

This is useful for model-risk and compliance review because subgroup behavior often matters as much as individual decision behavior. A system may appear reasonable at the individual level while producing problematic differences across groups.

The fairness module is intentionally framed as diagnostics, not certification. It is not proof of fairness, not a substitute for legal review, and not sufficient for regulated deployment. It is an inspection layer that helps teams decide where deeper review is needed.

## **10\. Enterprise integration path**

The package is built with local seams so that enterprise adapters can be added later without rewriting the core engine.

| Local implementation | Enterprise adapter path |
| :---- | :---- |
| API key auth | Okta, Auth0, SSO, API gateway authorization |
| Local file or SQLite audit storage | Postgres or governed audit database |
| JSON model configs | Managed model registry |
| JSON policy configs | Governed policy registry |
| Local evidence packs | Enterprise audit archive |
| Single-tenant default mode | Tenant-aware deployment boundary |
| CLI and FastAPI | Internal service or controlled review environment |

This is the correct pre-hosting pattern. The package does not pretend to be enterprise infrastructure. Instead, it defines the boundaries where enterprise infrastructure belongs.

That distinction matters. Fake enterprise code increases technical debt. Replaceable seams reduce it.

## **11\. Implementation and Verification Status (v0.4.0)** 

| Area | Current Status | Evidence |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Release baseline** | Updated | v0.4.0 |
| **Automated test suite** | Updated | 52 passed (python \-m pytest \-q) |
| **Core inference math** | Unchanged | Exact discrete DAG inference logic unchanged from reference design |
| **Reference demo behavior** | Unchanged | Default example risk probability remains 0.849375 |
| **Replay determinism** | Active | Replay contract checks enforce model/policy/version matching |
| **Audit integrity** | Active | Audit hash \+ chain verification implemented and tested |
| **Batch decision robustness** | Active | Row-level validation/errors; no silent partial-evidence decisions |

This status reflects implementation verification for the reference package and test harness. It is **not** a claim of production lending validation or regulatory certification.

## **11b. Public Financial Dataset Validation Results** The project now includes **public institutional loan-level validation** and **public complaint-data validation** paths using external public datasets.

### **Mortgage validation (Freddie Mac / Fannie Mae / HMDA-CFPB)**

* **Rows processed:** 30,000  
* **Accepted rows:** 30,000  
* **Rejected rows:** 0  
* **Decision distribution:** APPROVE 9,584 | REVIEW 4,368 | DECLINE 16,048  
* **Replay success rate:** 1.0  
* **Audit-chain verification:** true  
* **Evidence-pack mode:** sampled (1,000 rows)

### **CFPB complaint-data validation**

* **Rows processed:** 10,000 CFPB complaint rows  
* **Decision distribution:** APPROVE 0 | REVIEW 9,837 | DECLINE 163  
* **Replay success rate:** 1.0  
* **Audit-chain verification:** true

These results demonstrate end-to-end governance traceability on public financial datasets (decisioning, replay, audit-chain, and diagnostics). They remain **reference validation results**, not production validation, do not use customer data, and do not establish credit-eligibility policy or regulatory compliance.

## **12\. Limitations**

The package has been validated on public institutional datasets (Freddie Mac, Fannie Mae, HMDA/CFPB, CFPB complaint data). This is not production validation and does not establish credit-eligibility policy 

* The current release has hard boundaries.  
* The CPDs are illustrative. They are not learned from validated lending data.  
* The reference model is not a production credit model.  
* The package must not be used for real lending decisions or credit eligibility decisions.  
* The system is not proof of EU AI Act compliance.  
* The fairness diagnostics are not fairness certification.  
* The package has not been validated on customer data.  
* The package does not include enterprise SSO.  
* The package does not include hosted deployment.  
* The package does not include production-grade storage.  
* The package does not replace legal, compliance, model-risk, or adverse-action review.

Any real-world deployment would require validated data, domain expert review, model-risk approval, fairness testing, security review, policy governance, monitoring, incident procedures, and jurisdiction-specific legal analysis.

## **13\. Conclusion**

`causal-credit-risk-engine` demonstrates a practical architecture for causal explainability and audit-grade AI governance. Its value is not the reference credit-risk model. Its value is the governance pattern.

The pattern is: define causal assumptions explicitly, separate model inference from decision policy, generate counterfactuals, preserve audit records, support deterministic replay, and protect decision traces with integrity checks.

That pattern can extend beyond credit risk. The same architecture can apply anywhere an organization needs to explain, replay, inspect, and challenge high-risk AI decisions.

The contribution is the governance architecture: causal reasoning, replay, integrity, and auditability as one decision-trace pipeline.

**References**

1. Pearl, J. (2009). *Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference* (2nd ed.). [Cambridge University Press & Assessment](https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/causality/B0046844FAE10CBF274D4ACBDAEB5F5B?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
2. Wachter, S., Mittelstadt, B., & Russell, C. (2017/2018). *Counterfactual Explanations Without Opening the Black Box: Automated Decisions and the GDPR*. Harvard Journal of Law & Technology / [SSRN](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3063289.&utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
3. Kusner, M. J., Loftus, J. R., Russell, C., & Silva, R. (2017). *Counterfactual Fairness*. NeurIPS / [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06856?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
4. Chou, Y.-L., Moreira, C., Bruza, P., Ouyang, C., & Jorge, J. (2021). *Counterfactuals and Causability in Explainable Artificial Intelligence*. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.04244?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
5. Karimi, A.-H., Schölkopf, B., & Valera, I. (2021). *Algorithmic Recourse: From Counterfactual Explanations to Interventions*. [Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44387-026-00095-1?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
6. Baron, S. (2023). *Explainable AI and Causal Understanding: Counterfactual Approaches Considered*. *Minds and Machines*. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11023-023-09637-x?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
7. Sánchez, P., Voisey, J. P., Xia, T., Watson, H. I., O’Neil, A. Q., & Tsaftaris, S. A. (2022). *Causal Machine Learning for Healthcare and Precision Medicine*. *Royal Society Open Science* / [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9346354/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
8. Jiao, L., et al. (2024). *Causal Inference Meets Deep Learning: A Comprehensive Survey*.   
   *Research*. [Science Advances](https://spj.science.org/doi/10.34133/research.0467?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
9. Bienefeld, N., et al. (2023). *Solving the Explainable AI Conundrum by Bridging Clinicians’ Needs and Developers’ Goals*. *npj Digital Medicine*. [Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-023-00837-4?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
10. Fink, M. (2025). *Human Oversight under Article 14 of the EU AI Act*. [SSRN](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5147196&utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
11. European Union. (2024). *Artificial Intelligence Act, Article 13: Transparency and Provision of Information to Deployers*. [StreamLex](https://streamlex.eu/articles/aia-en-art-13/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
12. European Union. (2024). *Artificial Intelligence Act, Article 14: Human Oversight*. [Artificial Intelligence Act](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/14/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
13. Bank for International Settlements. (2025). *How Regulators Can Address AI Explainability*. Financial Stability Institute. [Bank for International Settlements](https://www.bis.org/fsi/fsipapers24.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
